aids.title: Extendible Hashing: GLD
aids.content: G = Global depth (directory width), L = Local depth (bucket-specific), D = Directory (pointer array). Remember: when G == L and overflow occurs, G grows and D doubles.
aids.forRemembering: The three key components of extendible hashing and the doubling condition

aids.title: Extendible Hashing as a Phone Book
aids.content: Think of the directory as a phone book index. Initially you look up names by first letter (1 bit = 2 entries). As sections overflow, you refine to two letters (2 bits = 4 entries). Only the overflowing section gets subdivided -- you do not rewrite the entire phone book.
aids.forRemembering: How directory doubling and bucket splitting work in extendible hashing

aids.title: Linear vs Extendible: No Directory Needed
aids.content: Linear hashing does NOT use a directory. It uses a split pointer that moves sequentially through buckets. Think: Linear = sequential splits, Extendible = directory-based splits. Linear hashing may use overflow chains temporarily until the split pointer reaches the overflowing bucket.
aids.forRemembering: The key structural difference between linear and extendible hashing
